clean_story:
Shadows as timers. Nature’s rhythms ruled.

The phrase "time before clocks" points to eras when humans tracked hours by the sun’s crawl, the moon’s phases, or a candle’s burn—not gears or digits.

A sundial’s shadow moves like a slow hand, creeping one body-length every 75 minutes (recorded in Egypt around 1500 BCE). At night, water clocks dripped sand-sized grains through holes; a full bowl marked a judge’s speech limit in ancient Athens. Monks lit knotted ropes: each knot burned for an hour, ringing a bell to signal prayer. The Maya aligned pyramids to cast serpent-shaped shadows on equinoxes, turning stone into calendars.

Without clocks, time stretched or shrank with tasks. A farmer’s "hour" expanded at harvest; a sailor’s contracted near storms. The first mechanical clock in 1300s Europe didn’t tell time—it chimed prayers, dividing days into equal slices for the first time.

Three related subjects are moon-based calendars, fire as timekeeper, and how trade routes spread hours.
